ads.author: Guy Martin
ads.title: Unique WATCHE from Mr. Jones
ads.description: IMPORTANT NOTE: If you want to see all of my Facebook Marketplace listings (Collectables, Fashion, Watches, Automobilia, Art, Household items etc.) when looking at a listing, look to the right side of the page with the listing details. Scroll down to find the seller information with my photo. Next, click on seller details, scroll to the bottom of that page and you'll see all of my listings. Our home is smoke free, pet, free, child free, and we do not wear outside shoes in our home. All items are in excellent condition unless otherwise specified. We live off Las Olas on Hendricks Isle. Cash only upon pick up. Mr. Jones Watch Ricochet $325 Get lost in time with the Ricochet watch. Ricochet is inspired by the world of the pinball arcade and was designed for us by Ryan Claytor. The three metallic robots, engrossed in the action of the pinball machine were each gilded by hand in a different metallic foil palladium, 20ct. citron gold and 22ct. rose gold. The scoreboard of the game shows you the hours and minutes. This watch has a jump hour movement, so you'll need to watch closely to see the hour change - blink and you'll miss it! Ricochet was a real labor of love to produce, each watch representing many hours of work by our small, talented team. • Medium / unisex size • Sapphire glass (hard-wearing) • TY2709 20 jewel automatic mechanical movement • Jump hour movement • Waterproof! Rated 5ATM • 316L stainless steel case • 18mm stainless steel strap About the Artist • Ryan Claytor • Ryan, who has a deep love for pinball, considers the links between the game and time: • “Pinball is essentially a game of timing: flip a fraction of a second too soon or too late and you'll miss your intended target. • In its more transcendent moments though, a game of pinball can almost mystically remove the player from any concept of time. • When engrossed in a game where shots are being hit, progress being made and previously locked achievements are realized, pinball has the capacity to transport the player out of time entirely. • Rest assured, though, that "Ricochet" will be a faithful temporal reminder once you resurface from this engrossing experience!” • Ryan Claytor is an award-winning American Comics Creator and professor at Michigan State University (MSU) where he teaches Comics Studio courses, heads the Comic Art and Graphic Novel interdisciplinary minor and directs the MSU Comics Forum. • Claytor creates non-fiction comics that, most recently, focus on the history of electro-mechanical coin-operated amusement devices (pinball and arcade games manufactured prior to the implementation of solid-state technology in 1978). • Additionally, he is the Host and Producer of the MSU Comic Art and Graphic Novel Podcast and designs custom neon signs.

Crisis Zone (1999) is an intense, action-packed arcade light-gun shooter by Namco and a spin-off of the Time Crisis series. Players control Claude McGarren, an elite STF agent, utilizing a mounted submachine gun (Steyr Mannlicher TMP) and a foot pedal system to take cover, reload, and destroy massive, highly destructible environments while battling terrorists in a 3D urban setting. Key Features and Gameplay: Unique Controller: Instead of a pistol, players use a mounted submachine gun with 40-round capacity and a laser sight. Cover Mechanism: A foot pedal allows players to take cover, acting as both a reload function and a shield against enemy fire. Destructible Environments: Players can destroy the environment, including shooting CDs off shelves, breaking glass, and destroying clothing racks. Campaign Structure: The game features three main stages (Subway, Hotel, Commercial Zone) that can be played in any order, followed by a final boss stage. Action-Oriented: The game is known for high-intensity, rapid-fire gameplay, often featuring faster enemy encounters than the standard Time Crisis. Crisis Zone is powered by Namco System 23 hardware and typically features 27-inch CRT or 50-inch projection displays.

ads.author: Andys Pinball
ads.title: RESTORED 1980 ATARI CENTIPEDE, NOS MONITOR, NEW ARTWORK, SERVICED GAME PCB
ads.description: This game is in AMAZING CONDITION, ESPECIALLY when you factor in that it is 46 years old. The game's restoration spared no expense and the game has been serviced/bullet proofed for years to come. The monitor was replaced with a NOS one (new old stock) and has a perfect picture with absolutely ZERO screen burn in. This is uncommon for this title because the high scores remain on the monitor during the game over/attract mode. Meaning, the only time the high scores aren't on the screen is when someone is actively playing a game. Most used Centipedes have terrible burn in, or the monitor has been replaced with an LCD which is substandard to the original CRT style monitor this one features. There are many Centipedes that come and go on eBay and FB, and there are even some listed online now. What you will find is that they are not original, most (if not all of them) are running a 60in1 knock-off board (they may say "we added a joystick and two more buttons so you can play Donkey Kong." Or "new hardware for reliability" or "monitor upgraded to an LCD.") First, a 60in1 is NOT an upgrade, neither is an LCD monitor. Both are downgrades. A 60in1 runs an emulated version of the game, and the biggest drawback is that it doesn't save the initials or all of the high scores. It will save the top score and it will disregard your initials entered, so the next time you turn on the game the initials will say EBD (Or EDB I forget.) Go ahead and find one online and look to see what the highest score initials are. Go ahead. See mine says DAD for the top 3 (so far!) The other thing that doesn't work properly when someone has replaced the original board with a 60in1 is that the player 1 and player 2 cone start lights don't blink as there is no way for the 60in1 to power them. It would seem to me that if you are looking for an "original Centipede" that you'd seek out the one that has the original hardware that has been serviced by someone who knows what they are doing. Don't get me wrong, 60in1 boards have their place. Just not as a solution to repairing an original classic that is this fantastic. What service has been performed on this you ask? I installed two new ram chips and a new crystal. I disassembled the board, the board was washed, dried, all socketed chips were removed, the chip legs were cleaned and DeOxit was applied before reassembly to ensure the chips have excellent continuity in their sockets. The edge connectors were cleaned and any flux from prior repairs was removed. The power supply was adjusted to output the correct voltages and the game was burned in for 30 hours to ensure reliability. You are welcome to pick this up at my home in S.E. Charlotte, NC 28226 or I can ship it to you. If you require shipping please send me your address so that I can get you a rate quote. If you have other coin operated toys, I will consider your trade (pinball or arcade game) +/- cash.
